title: Miracle on Ice Congressional Gold Medal Act
short_title: Miracle on Ice Congressional Gold Medal Act
plain_summary: This bill creates special gold medals to honor the 1980 United States men's Olympic ice hockey team. The team became famous for their "Miracle on Ice" when they beat the powerful Soviet Union team at the Winter Olympics. Congress wants to give them these medals because their victory was so important and inspiring to Americans. The medals will be put in museums for people to see, and copies can be made and sold to the public.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Miracle on Ice Congressional Gold Medal Act.

sections.title: Section 2 Findings
sections.text: Congress has found these facts about the nineteen eighty United States Olympic ice hockey team. The team competed in the Winter Olympics from February thirteenth to twenty fourth, nineteen eighty, in Lake Placid, New York. Team USA was made up of college players who defeated the Soviet Union team four to three on February twenty second, nineteen eighty. The Soviet Union team had been the defending Olympic champions. The nineteen eighty United States team included twenty players from various states including Minnesota, Massachusetts, Wisconsin, and Michigan. The game between the United States and Soviet Union was shown on television later that day and drew over thirty four million viewers. People called it a miracle because college players beat a Soviet team that had won four gold medals in a row since nineteen sixty four. Team USA then defeated Finland four to two in their final game to win the gold medal. This was their first gold medal in mens ice hockey since nineteen sixty. Herb Brooks coached the team. He had been cut from the nineteen sixty United States Olympic team that won gold. Brooks motivated his players and built a team based on hard work and believing in themselves and their teammates. The tournament happened during a difficult time for the United States. The country was dealing with high prices, expensive gas, hostages held in Iran, and tension with the Soviet Union because of their invasion of Afghanistan. The Miracle on Ice became a turning point for ice hockey in the United States. Sports Illustrated called it the greatest sports moment of the twentieth century. The win brought ice hockey to newspaper front pages everywhere and opened doors for American players to join the National Hockey League. Since nineteen eighty, interest in ice hockey in the United States has grown greatly. USA Hockey registrations have increased by nearly four hundred percent from one hundred thirty six thousand to over five hundred sixty four thousand. The number of National Hockey League players from the United States has increased from seventy two in nineteen eighty to two hundred forty five in twenty twenty four.

sections.title: Section 3 Congressional Gold Medals
sections.text: The Speaker of the House of Representatives and the President pro tempore of the Senate (a senior member who leads the Senate when the Vice President is not there) will arrange for Congress to give three gold medals to honor the members of the nineteen eighty United States Olympic Mens Ice Hockey Team. These medals recognize their extraordinary achievement at the Winter Olympics where college players defeated the powerful Soviet hockey team in the historic Miracle on Ice. This victory lifted American spirits during the Cold War, inspired many generations, and changed ice hockey in the United States. The Secretary of the Treasury will create gold medals with proper symbols, designs, and writing as the Secretary decides. After the medals are awarded, one gold medal will go to the Lake Placid Olympic Center in Lake Placid, New York, where it will be displayed and available for research as needed. One gold medal will go to the United States Hockey Hall of Fame Museum in Eveleth, Minnesota, where it will be displayed and available for research as needed. One gold medal will go to the United States Olympic and Paralympic Museum in Colorado Springs, Colorado, where it will be displayed and available for research as needed.

sections.title: Section 4 Duplicate Medals
sections.text: The Secretary of the Treasury may make and sell bronze copies of the gold medals at a price that covers all the costs. These costs include labor, materials, metal dies (tools used to stamp the medals), use of machinery, and overhead expenses.

sections.title: Section 5 Status of Medals
sections.text: All medals made under this law are considered national medals according to chapter fifty one of title thirty one of the United States Code (a section of federal law about medals). For the purposes of sections fifty one thirty four and fifty one thirty six of title thirty one of the United States Code, all medals made under this law will be considered numismatic items (collectible coins and medals).

sections.title: Section 6 Authority to Use Fund Amounts and Proceeds of Sale
sections.text: The government can charge the United States Mint Public Enterprise Fund (a special account that pays for mint operations) for any amounts needed to pay for making the medals under this law. Money received from selling the bronze duplicate medals will be put back into the United States Mint Public Enterprise Fund.
